text.title: Cognitive Walkthrough Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules anchoring the cognitive-walkthrough methodology, each grounded in a source.

rule.statement: Each step MUST answer 4 questions: (Q1) Will the user try to achieve the right effect? (Q2) Will they notice the correct action is available? (Q3) Will they associate the action with the effect? (Q4) Will they see progress / feedback?
rule.rationale: Skipping any of the four leaves an undiscovered learnability gap; the rubric is the methodology's whole contribution.

rule.statement: A canonical correct-action sequence MUST be defined before evaluation; mid-walkthrough rewriting of the sequence is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Evaluating against a moving target produces incomparable results across evaluators.

rule.statement: Evaluators MUST adopt the first-time-user frame (no prior product knowledge); evaluator product expertise MUST be explicitly bracketed.
rule.rationale: Expert users skip steps that confuse novices; the frame discipline is the methodology's reliability lever.

rule.statement: Walkthrough MUST be run by >=2 evaluators independently; results merged via discussion.
rule.rationale: Single-evaluator walkthroughs amplify individual blind spots; 2+ independents catch more issues.

rule.statement: Every finding MUST carry a severity (catastrophic / major / minor / cosmetic) tied to a fix prioritisation rubric.
rule.rationale: Unprioritised findings stall remediation; classification mirrors heuristic-evaluation discipline.

rule.statement: If the Skip-If preconditions hold, the methodology MUST be skipped and the caller routed to the linked alternative.
rule.rationale: Applying this machinery to the wrong workflow burns tokens without producing a usable artefact.
